previewTitle.value: Fernando Trujillo: For the love of river dolphins

title.value: Fernando Trujillo: For the love of river dolphins

subtitle.value: Rolex national geographic explorer of the year

text.value:
Colombian marine biologist Fernando Trujillo is the 2024 Rolex National Geographic Explorer of the Year for his work protecting the Amazon’s pink dolphins and the river system that is their home.

Fernando Trujillo, 2024 Rolex National Geographic Explorer of the Year, has never had any doubt about the way he wanted to spend his life. A voyage with his grandfather to the village of Puerto Carreño on the banks of the Orinoco River in Colombia inspired his decades of research into freshwater dolphins.

“I was in love with the dolphins. Even now, 37 years after, it’s amazing to find dolphins in the jungle,” Trujillo says. “The dolphins are a key species for people to create empathy and to understand all the problems that the Amazon is facing.”

The Rolex National Geographic Explorer of the Year is awarded annually to a member of the National Geographic Explorer community who shines a light on important issues and discoveries as well as the challenges facing the planet, and who inspires the world to take action for a more sustainable future.

Describing the award as an “immense honour”, Trujillo says: “I have seen several explorers I greatly admire receive this award and I still can’t believe that I could be following in their footsteps.” Recalling how many times frustration and exhaustion have driven him to the verge of giving up, he says: “Acknowledgements like this really fill me with joy and hope, and I am deeply grateful.”

Alongside his research, Trujillo coordinates international commitments to protect the dolphins. He was a key player when the 2023 Global Agreement to Protect River Dolphins was signed by 11 countries in Asia and South America.
Acknowledgements like this really fill me with joy and hope, and I am deeply grateful.

Since the 1980s, freshwater dolphin populations have dropped by 73 per cent due to fishing, pollution and development; with climate change they face the additional threat of warming waters.

The award amplifies his work in the international community, he notes. “It creates multiple possibilities to work with governments, companies and many environmental organizations around the conservation of the Amazon.”

Trujillo is proud of what has been achieved working with local communities to create a conservation programme for the Amazon through its dolphins. “It has even inspired other people to do similar things in the rivers of Asia.”

He is also a member of the Rolex and National Geographic Perpetual Planet Amazon Expedition, a multi-year exploration of the freshwater ecosystem critical to sustaining the world’s largest rainforest. The data collected by seven teams of explorers across different ecosystems will allow a detailed analysis of the health of the region and the most appropriate solutions to be implemented for its preservation. For his part, Trujillo has surveyed 1,270 kilometres of rivers across four countries, estimating that there are 1,366 pink dolphins.

Looking to the future, he hopes to continue his work by further collaborating with local communities and other explorers, securing more resources and being more effective in bringing “innovative solutions to the problems facing this region”.

pageTitle.value: Perpetual Planet Amazon Expedition - Through the Tributaries

pageCaption.value: Fernando Trujillo and María Jimena Valderrama are captivated by the charismatic dolphins swimming throughout the Amazon River basin. With support from the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative and the National Geographic Society, their latest international study, an assessment of the dolphin’s health and its habitat, has yielded encouraging results.

metadescription.value: By studying the Amazon's river dolphins, Fernando Trujillo and María Jimena Valderrama are gleaning insight into the wider river ecosystem. More on rolex.org.
